data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery's Separation Rally Still Has Legs - Double Digit Upside Possible
data.description:
WBD Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Rally, Thanks To The Massive Gap To Book Value

We previously covered Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) in March 2024, discussing how they had delivered the D2C inflection as promised, as observed in the positive bottom-line, the growing advertising opportunities, and the expanding streaming share.

Combined with the management's new content initiatives, we had believed that the media company was likely to deliver a higher consumer engagement/ ARPU growth/ advertising share, albeit with near-term risks arising from the impacted Free Cash Flow generation and the hefty balance sheet.

WBD 1Y Stock Price

Since that Buy rating, WBD has further rallied by +90.2%, with it well outperforming the wider market at +20% and many of its streaming peers in varying degrees.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the legacy company's ability to increasingly monetize its streaming offerings while growing the subscriber base, despite the deteriorating D2C share at 6.1% as of July 2025 (-0.3 points MoM/ -1.3 YoY).

These have been observed in the growing streaming advertising revenues of $282M in FQ2'25 (+18.9% QoQ/ +17.5% YoY), with it apparent that their intensified monetization strategies through the expanded global partnerships with numerous Multichannel Video Programming Distributors [MVPD] and non MVPD players have already delivered promising early results.

This is especially since WBD has been reporting a growing D2C subscriber base to 125.7M (+2.7% QoQ/ +21.6% YoY) albeit negated by the lower ARPUs of $7.14 (+0.4% QoQ/ -10.7% YoY) - with the net effect still being positive as observed in the higher D2C Distribution revenues of $2.41B (+3.8% QoQ/ +9.5% YoY).

Combined with the promising inflection of its D2C adj EBITDA margins at 12% in H1'25 (+12.4 points YoY), we can understand why the market has gotten exuberant surrounding its future D2C prospects.

This is significantly aided by the upcoming separation of the Streaming/ Studios and the Global Linear Network into two publicly traded companies - a highly strategic decision, in our opinion.

This is especially since WBD's Streaming & Studios segment is already reporting richer adj EBITDA performance at $1.33B (+241% YoY) at margins of 13.9% in H1'25 (+9.8 points YoY), despite the flat revenues at $9.53B (inline YoY).

This strategy effectively allows WBD's Streaming & Studios segment to be valued nearer to its peers or the market leader, Netflix (NFLX), while isolating the impact of the secular decline of linear TVs to the former's Global Linear Network's fading advertising prospects at overall revenues of $9.57B (-7.8% YoY) and deteriorating adj EBITDA margin performance of 34.5% in H1'25 (-4.9 points YoY).

With the separation expected to be completed by mid 2026, we believed that the WBD management has unlocked great value for long-term shareholders indeed, allowing both segments to separately realize "greater strategic flexibility and focus so that each business can invest in and pursue its operational and financial goals" while "attract a shareholder base aligned with its growth prospects and financial profiles."

These reasons may also be why the market has rallied around the media company's promising prospects upon separation, as observed in the stock's eyewatering rally by +63% since the April 2025 bottom and by +29.9% since the announcement of the separation in June 09, 2025 (prior to the release of the PSKY offer).

A similar synergy has been acknowledged by its competitor as well, with Paramount Skydance (NASDAQ:PSKY) seemingly keen on "preparing a majority cash bid for the entertainment giant" before the upcoming separation.

This development is unsurprising indeed, since it allows the two legacy giants to merge their IPs while expanding their appeal to a wider base of subscribers, with these efforts likely to be top/ bottom-line accretive.

At the same time, WBD is likely to benefit from the deal, attributed to the potentially attractive cash offer and the accelerated deleveraging process moving forward, with it perhaps exemplifying why the stock has enjoyed an outsized one day rally by +28.9%.

Even so, the path may be fraught with challenges, depending on shareholder and regulatory approval - especially since the report has yet to be substantiated by both parties, barring which, it may simply be a market rumor.

As a result, we urge readers to observe the developing situation a little closer before making a decision.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, WBD is still expected to be generate rich adj EBITDA and Free Cash Flow performance moving forward, with it building upon the H1'25 performance at $4.05B (+4.1% YoY) and $1B (-26.4% YoY), respectively, with it underscoring their ability to drive profitable operations upon separation and hopefully, renewed growth for the Streaming & Studios segment.

WBD Valuations

These reasons may also be why WBD has been able to trade at a relatively stable FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 7.26x for the past year, not too far from the 5Y mean of 7.61x.

If anything, readers may also look forward to further upgrading in its valuations nearer to its streaming peers, including:

  • NFLX at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 37.97x with a 3Y adj. EBITDA growth estimates at a CAGR of +22.4%,
  • Roku (ROKU) at 32.64x/ +40.6%,
  • Alphabet (GOOG) at 16.11x/ +15.8%,
  • Amazon (AMZN) at 15.17x/ +17.6%,
  • PSKY at 9.48x/ +4.5%, and
  • Comcast (CMCSA) at 5.72x/ +0.2%, respectively,

with it apparent that WBD remains inherently cheap at FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 7.26x if the Streaming & Studios segment is able to sustain the H1'25 adj EBITDA growth profile at +241% YoY.

These developments imply that the stock may potentially offer a rich capital appreciation prospects despite the recent double digits rally, pending further clarity on the separation process and most importantly, the debt division.

Our caution is attributed to WBD's immense albeit moderating net debt levels of $29.75B (-11.3% QoQ/ -20.3% YoY/ -34.2% from the peak FY 2022 levels of $45.26B), with it apparent that a higher debt level may trigger a prolonged deleveraging cadence and consequently, higher interest expenses and impacted bottom-lines.

These critical developments are also why we urge interested investors to closely monitor the mechanics of the upcoming separation, since the upward re-rating in the Streaming & Studios segment hinges upon a rich bottom-line.

So, Is WBD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

WBD 3Y Stock Price

For now, we can understand why WBD has optimistically broken out of numerous resistance levels while running away from the 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages, thanks to the ongoing inflection observed in the D2C segment's bottom-lines and the tailwinds arising from the upcoming separation/ PSKY's cash offer.

This is especially since the media company is still cheap against its growing Book Value per share of $14.57 in FQ2'25 (+6.5% QoQ/ +3.9% YoY) while being inherently undervalued compared to our fair value estimates of $23.50.

The latter is based on the annualized H1'25 adj EBITDA of $8.1B, the last shares outstanding of 2.49B, the annualized H1'25 adj EBITDA per share of $3.25, along with the current FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 7.26x (not too far from the 5Y mean of 7.61x, yet still cheap compared to the peer group mean of ~19x).

A similar conclusion may also be derived against the annualized H1'25 net income per share of $0.90, the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 24.28x (not too far from the 5Y mean of 24.09x, yet cheap compared to the peer group mean of ~27x), and the resultant fair value estimates of $21.80, with it apparent that its ongoing rally still has legs.

WBD's Likely To Be Durable Upward Momentum

Even so, the outsized one day rally has triggered WBD's mixed technical indicators, with the Relative Strength Index [RSI] appearing inflated against the still moderate trading volumes (compared to historical peaks in red), with it implying its rather exuberant upward momentum compared to the prior pessimism levels observed in 2022/ 2023.

This is significantly aided by the moderating short interest volumes by -32% on a YoY basis, with it signaling that the worst of its prior headwinds may already be behind us, as the media company achieves improved operating scale upon expanding adoption/ monetization cadence while enjoying a renewed bullish support from the upcoming separation.

While we may reiterate our Buy rating for the WBD stock, it comes with the caveat that investors wait out the PSKY exuberance to be moderated first, preferably nearer to its prior July 2025 peak of $13s/ Book Value per share of $14s for an improved margin of safety.

Despite the still rich double digits upside potential and the much needed bounce at the recent August 2025 floor of $11.20s ranges, some prudence may be warranted in the near-term.

data.title: I Own The Gas Pump With Up To 10% Yields
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Sometimes I drive my wife crazy when we are traveling to go visit family or just run errands by constantly pointing out different companies or areas that we pass by that provide us with the income we so greatly enjoy from our portfolio.

One of my favorites I like to point out is when we stop to purchase fuel. For years, I have been getting a silent rebate from the gas pump. I'm a shareholder of fuel distribution companies; so, when I visit the gas pump or you visit the gas pump from across thousands of different gas stations, I am collecting money back.

The fuel distribution network is largely misunderstood. When you see a sign on the top of a gas station, you can sometimes be misled to believe that the company owns that specific location. Although, often it's a franchise that owns a location and sells that brand of fuel. Rarely does the company that sells you fuel transport it from their refinery to the gas station. There is a middleman who does the logistics, the shipping, and the movement. The middleman gets a cut of every gallon that they ship. Within the United States, there are various fuel distribution names that exist, and these different names readily move fuel from point A to point B. For some, fuel distribution is all they do; for others, it was a primary aspect of their business, but now, while it may still be important, it has become a smaller part of their business.

Today, I'd like to take a quick look at the three major fuel distribution investment opportunities and explore how you can also receive a rebate every time you fill up. Let's dive in.

Pick #1: SUN – Yield 7%

Sunoco LP (SUN) is an MLP (Master Limited Partnership) that focuses on midstream opportunities. Originally, SUN was solely focused on fuel distribution and convenience store ownership or leasing. However, as they've expanded in size, they've also expanded their focus to more traditional midstream opportunities. SUN is a subsidiary of the well-known midstream behemoth Energy Transfer (ET) and pays incentive distribution rights upwards to their parent company.

Note: SUN issues a K-1 at tax time. Source

During a period when ET focused on deleveraging rather than empire building, it shifted more emphasis to empire building through its SUN subsidiary. Consequently, we saw the successful purchase of Nustar and its terminal business, as well as ownership of various pipelines being sold from ET to SUN.

Currently, SUN is under a continued growth focus by trying to buy out Parkland, a large field distribution company located within the United States, with business also in the Caribbean. This fuel distribution buyout is expected to be highly accredited to SUN unitholders and provide a way for Canadians to have exposure to SUN without the onerous taxation faced by international investors when investing in a master limited partnership.

SUN also has a renewed focus on growing their distribution – something that had been stalled for years but now sees regular growth once more and has strong trailing coverage from their free cash flow. Their trailing 12-month coverage as of last quarter was 1.9x, meaning that there is healthy room for them to not only invest in growth, but also to continue to raise their distribution to shareholders.

Pick #2: GLP – Yield 5.7%

Global Partners LP (GLP) is another MLP that focuses on fuel distribution, as well as other midstream opportunities.

Note: GLP issues a K-1 at tax time.

GLP was started originally as a family business that has since grown into a very successfully run, large-scale operation. Historically, GLP has grown through acquisitions of various convenience stores or smaller fuel distribution businesses to expand its influence and reach. Source

Currently, over 60% of the profit margin that exists within GLP comes directly from fuel distribution or convenience store ownership and leasing. Recently, however, GLP has also been focusing on terminal opportunities and, in the last few years, has made multiple large acquisitions of terminals from oil majors or other oil companies. In essence, this allows them to have a bunkering effect on the fuel that they purchase from refineries, which they then eventually ship out to their own stores or sell on the wholesale market. If you're able to store more fuel, you can ride out the price variations. Also, you can charge for storing fuel for others.

GLP has been on a steady path of distribution growth and recently announced their 15th consecutive distribution hike. GLP is extremely conservatively run, and if they do not have the ability to cover the distribution over the long term, they are willing to reduce it or cut it back to then resume growth at a healthy rate. As such, management has been proactive in trying to grow the distribution when they're able to do so from the healthy operation of the company.

With so much of their revenue coming from fuel distribution and convenience store operations, GLP is more exposed to the seasonality of fuel demand than the SUN would be. Fuel demand always peaks in the travel time frames, typically the second and third quarters, and then dies down in the colder months or the schooling months. This is something to be aware of when you're watching the earnings of any of these fuel distribution companies. Seasonality always impacts companies with more exposure to fuel distribution than to midstream opportunities. GLP is more impacted by this than SUN would be.

Pick #3: CAPL – Yield 10.2%

CrossAmerica Partners LP (CAPL) is an MLP that owns and operates convenience stores as well as distributes fuel to third-party locations.

Note: CAPL issues a K-1 at tax time.

Of the three names that we have examined today, CAPL is by far the smallest and the most concentrated on fuel distribution and convenience store ownership – that is all CAPL does!

Unlike GLP, which has a heating fuel business and terminals, and unlike SUN, which has terminals and pipelines on top of their fuel distribution, CAPL is a dedicated, focused fuel distribution name only. As such, CAPL experiences the greatest amount of seasonality when it comes to fuel distribution.

CAPL has been on a focus to reduce its leverage. Growth is currently secondary to ensuring they maintain a strong portfolio of holdings and reduce leverage. Any cuts to the prime interest rate will benefit their ability to reduce the cost of their active debt and allow them to pay down that debt faster and resume growth once more. We shouldn't overlook the CAPL simply because it's the smallest and most seasonal. Its yield currently is the highest, and they have a strong dedication to maintaining that yield while reducing their debt. However, CAPL's distribution coverage is the tightest as well; its 12-month trailing distribution coverage is exactly 1x, leaving little room for management error.

During this most recent quarter, CAPL was able to cut down on their debt by $50 million. This was mainly due to selling off locations that were not operating profitably for them to operate the convenience store. But these sales also largely included the ability for them to continue distributing fuel to that location under the new ownership. This means that they will still receive some level of cash flow from these locations, but with a massive reduction in operational costs tied to them. The debt that CAPL was able to pay down should reduce its annual interest expense by approximately $3 million. This should help bolster distribution coverage as well.

CAPL is definitely the smallest and riskiest option amongst these three, but it provides the highest yield. Investors who are interested in CAPL should note that distribution growth is unlikely until the company completes its leverage reduction process and sees additional portfolio holding growth to provide more revenue to increase the distribution in the future.

Conclusion

Fuel distribution may not be the most exciting sector to invest in or talk about. To be honest, if you were to walk up to someone that you know and ask them what they thought about fuel distribution as a sector, they probably won't have many cognitive thoughts because they haven't spent much time thinking about it.

But fuel distribution can be a highly successful and highly rewarding sector for an investor. It also falls well within the realm of the type of investing I like to do: To get money from every single aspect of my life. Go to the doctor's office? I want to get paid by the healthcare sector. Go to the grocery store? I like to make some money off of something that's on the shelf. Go to the gas station? I want a rebate.

When we look at the total return of GLP, SUN, and CAPL when compared to the general market (SPY), using the SPY as a proxy, we can see that over the long term, fuel distributors have provided extremely strong total returns – even besting the market.

When it comes to retirement, the simplest sources of income can often be the most rewarding and most exciting. Every single person who drives into the office burns some type of fuel, and when they're burning that fuel, you might as well receive some money from them doing what they have to do every single day. Historically, the need or requirement to buy gasoline to fund your vehicle was called the commuter tax because it was just a price that workers had to pay to make it to the office to earn money. Today, you can take that commuter tax and transform it into dollars in your pocket from the generous cash flow that these various opportunities provide you. Perhaps the next time you stop at the gas station, you can look at the gas pump, look at your loved one, and say that you're getting a rebate just like Rida Morwa.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Salesforce: AI ROI Still Falls Short Of Expectations
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The return of the AI dilemma is present again because of the Q2 FY26 (July 2025) results of Salesforce (NYSE:CRM). I want to point out one clarification: I don't question that this revolutionary technology will deliver a strong return on investment, but I do believe those returns don't meet the market expectations.

This said, let's analyze Salesforce, which I think fits perfectly in line with this personal analysis, and the upside of its share price, at least that has 2 digits on the EPS, which I see as very limited. Anyway, the fundamentals of the business are solid, and the possibility of its shares rebounding after a difficult 2025, still exists. I don't think this is a good moment to sell, given the stock is already down 27% year-to-date and still has room to recover, but at the same time, I don't think that this is a good moment to buy because I don't see a rebound on its shares. The holding position seems to be the better option.

Q2: Strong Results, Elevated Expectations

Salesforce’s second quarter beat the expectations of the market, regarding the revenue estimated by 1% to reach $10.24 billion, after suffering a little step back in Q1 FY26 (April 2025), which I highlighted in the chart below. Revenues are now up 10% year-over-year. It's worth mentioning that Salesforce continues to deliver solid growth, though I wouldn’t say they are spectacular.

The development of Data Cloud and Argentforce, the core of its AI, is still exceptional, with its annual income of $1.2 billion, with a YoY growth of 120%. Since the development of Argentforce last year, the company has signed 6000 deals and more than 12,500 in total, and an inter-quarter growth of 60% in the number of clients, that moved from Argentforce’s pilot test to productive use.

These strong results were also reflected in net income, which closed with a 32% year-over-year increase to $1.8 billion. The outlook is that, barring any surprises, the company will maintain net income levels between $6.5 billion and $7 billion for the entire year of 2025. Return on equity for shareholders, meanwhile, was $2.6 billion for the quarter, down from $4.7 billion for the same period last year.

EPS, meanwhile, left a positive impression, closing at $2.91, well above the $2.78 estimate, and even raising the full-year estimate to $11.33-$11.37 from the previous estimate of $11.27-$11.33. But beware -and here comes the first brake it believes the markets didn't like- the company flattened its EPS estimate for the next quarter at $2.84. That is, with a current EPS of $2.91, the company should have strong momentum heading into Q3 FY26 (Oct 2025) to prevent the metric from falling quarter-over-quarter. The outlook also moderated for the full-year operating margin, which fell from 21.6% to 21.2%.

In short, I think the results left two key messages. The positive message: the business is efficient and solid. The uncertain message: both Q3 and the fiscal year saw a decline in growth expectations.

When Will Stocks Rebound Again?

I know this may seem like an excessively detailed analysis of quarter-by-quarter earnings, but given the volatility in tech stock prices, knowing when to sell a stock is everything in an investment strategy that bets only on price. Now, those who already have a long position in Salesforce may interpret this differently. "I don't play short; I bought Salesforce long, and that's how I've made millions in tech," you might say. And I think it's fantastic that you're making the right investment. If you bought Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), or Meta Platforms (META) in the last three years, you've certainly made a profit. But in the case of Salesforce, the situation is different.

I understand that the company doesn't play in the big leagues of the seven tech megastars, but its business model is solid, stable, and its client base is, in my opinion, extraordinary. But unfortunately, Salesforce faces very strong competition in its cloud services markets, especially from a "Group of 7" company, Microsoft (MSFT), a company I also analyzed in a recent article. To put the differences in scale into perspective, MSFT's Azure line had revenues of $75 billion in its last reported fiscal year.

In this regard, I would like to briefly show, on the one hand, Salesforce's performance in recent years, represented by uncertain fluctuations in the stock price, and on the other, how high the price would have to go to 1) recover the investment if you have a position taken in the last year or so, and 2) what the upside potential is if we choose to buy today.

For the first point, let's start with a graph of the total return the company generated over the last three years compared to its competitors and two benchmark ETFs, such as SPY and XLK.

I'm not including Palantir here because I consider it a special bull case, also not justified by reality. In this chart, the 60% return over the last three years isn't bad, but it's not the best either. However, if we zoom in on this year's performance, the scenario becomes more complex.

While most tech stocks recovered from the first-quarter shock following the impact of tariffs and general uncertainty, Salesforce continued its price decline/stagnation. The all-time high price reached, both at the end of 2024 and in January 2025, was between $360 and $365, about 50% below current prices. According to EPS projections, and assuming the P/E of 23x doesn't continue to decline as it has been, that peak price would not be reached until 2029.

AI demands more results

It's truly difficult to reach the bar Wall Street sets for tech companies, and Salesforce serves as an example to me that this bar, currently represented by the AI ​​craze, is not only set on Big Tech but also on other long-standing market players that have a solid business model but whose expectations are sky-high. In other words, the just pay for the sinners. Some other examples of companies affected by this "craze" include Oracle, SAP, Palantir, or even AppLovin. But all of these have generated higher returns than Salesforce.

I believe Salesforce is a special case. It trades at multiples lower than the average for its last five years, and there are even signs of undervaluation in the PEG (30% lower than the sector median) and P/CF (9% lower than the sector median). All of this is reflected in a price that, as we saw, is far from its all-time high.

I believe this is because the market isn't entirely convinced by the return on investment from AI. Mind you, I believe the same thing is happening to big tech companies, but in Salesforce's case, it can't show returns of 1,000% over three years like NVDA. What does it need to show, then? Precisely, that much-desired return. As I explained in an article about META, this goal is difficult to demonstrate, although the company's CEO, Marc Benioff, is very good, in my opinion, at making visible what AI is really achieving in his business.

During the conference call, Benioff took the time to explain in detail how revolutionary AI is in the process of optimizing customer performance. He shared anecdotes and gave practical examples for several of his major clients, such as DIRECTV, PenFed, Under Armour, Reddit, and Williams Sonoma.

I've never been more excited about anything in my entire career. We're about to get into it. I've spent weeks on the road this summer meeting with CEOs, CIOs and frontline teams. And one thing is extremely clear to me, every single one of our customers is becoming an agentic enterprise. It's a huge vision for the future -- it's a huge vision for the future of business and really, it's a huge vision for the future of Salesforce.

But this isn't just an upgrade. This isn't simply just some automating some existing business process these agentic enterprises. Well, for Salesforce, it's certainly true. It's a complete transformation. And for our customers, the agentic enterprise is a complete reinvention in many cases of who they are and what their potential is. It's a shift from traditional hierarchies to reshaping the entire company, from busy work to orchestrating workflows, from siloed teams to seamless collaboration, from clicking and routing to natural conversations and hours are shrinking to seconds, employees and customers are being augmented.

It has also shown this in clear numbers. For example, Salesforce replaced a workforce of 4,000 employees with AI agents while maintaining its high score in customer service satisfaction. As we've shown before, another practical and concrete example is the increase in Data Cloud revenue, which is growing at a rate of 120% YoY driven by AI.

However, not everything is rosy. I fully understand, as I mentioned at the beginning, that AI is a revolutionary tool for efficiency and optimization, but the company's growth, in my opinion, is not extraordinary. Investors betting on the AI ​​race might ask themselves, "Is this the maximum return Salesforce can deliver by getting the most out of AI?" Clearly, reducing human employment reduces costs, and Data Cloud, if it represents $1.2 billion, represents just 3% of total TTM revenue, which is $39 billion. Take MSFT's $75 billion Azure; this represents 27% of its total TTM revenue of $281 billion. In short, AI demands more results, and Salesforce can deliver them, but I don't think it can translate them into impressive profit growth.

Valuation

To properly value the company, I decided to look at its strong metrics; that is, those that are allegedly evidencing a clear undervaluation in the price, implying a typical buy rating from the latest Seeking Alpha analysts who have covered the stock. Of the last 10 analysts who covered the stock, 9 rated it buy or strong buy. I understand this because if we look at the metrics table, the PEG and P/E ratios suggest a cheap stock relative to the average over the past 5 years, where the GAAP [TTM] P/E soared an average of 180 times earnings.

The reading, in my opinion, is the other way around. The price has inflated disproportionately in recent years and is now in a correction and fair valuation phase. To calculate this, I used precisely these metrics: a forward-looking PEG of 1.28x and a forward-looking P/E of 22.1x.

In line with my thesis, the expected growth rate is low compared to market expectations, and given that the company is already adjusting growth downward in the third quarter, I think it's important to look at this metric over the long term. The expected annual growth rate of diluted EPS is 15.6%, so the base case with a PEG of 1.28x will continue to lower the P/E and therefore the share price. In the following table, I show all possible scenarios based on an estimated EPS of $11.34.

PEG FWD EPS Growth FWD P/E EPS Target Price Upside
1.28x 11.00% 14.1x $12.60 $177 -29.8%
1.28x 13.00% 14.6x $12.83 $212 -13.7%
1.28x 15.60% 19.2x $13.12 $251 -0.1%
1.28x 17.34% 22.2x $13.31 $295 +17.2%
1.28x 19.00% 24.3x $13.49 $328 +30.1%
... ... ... ... ... ...
1.28x 30.00% 38.4x $14.74 $566 +124.6%

The target price varies according to the growth rate, from which I construct the P/E and EPS to arrive at different possible scenarios. If we look at the EPS growth estimates in the Seeking Alpha Earnings section, they never exceed 15% in any year, so I consider scenarios above that rate optimistic. I believe the valuation is fair at current prices and therefore choose to hold.

Clarification: I added a hypothetical growth rate of 30% because it's the only one that comes close to the company's average P/E over the last 5 years, demonstrating once again, in my opinion, how inflated the stock has become recently.

Risks

I would like to add what risks the company may face, beyond what has already been explained regarding competition and market sensitivity regarding its race for AI. When general economic conditions worsen, due to global uncertainties such as the issue of tariffs or due to continued high-interest rates, companies tend to be more cautious about expanding technology spending, including cloud services in general.

Another risk for the company is its exposure to international markets such as Asia Pacific (representing approximately 10% of revenue), as well as regulatory risks in Europe with the GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), which can impose fines for violating user privacy, even when suffering from cyberattacks.

Conclusion

In summary, the company offers a solid business model, with proper implementation of AI as a tool that boosts profits and enables its customers to be more efficient. However, the results fall short of the high expectations of the tech sector, and future growth prospects are weakening. This implies that its stock price, although far from its peak, I believe shows a justified valuation.

data.title: Capital City Bank: Raising The EPS Estimate After Earnings Surprise, But Maintaining A Hold Rating
data.description:
I adopted a hold rating on Capital City Bank Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:CCBG) in June 2025 in my article. However, since then, the stock price has rallied strongly by 15%. The price surge is mostly attributable to the second quarter’s earnings results, which beat my expectations mostly because of good expense control. I’m not the only one CCBG surprised, as the company has consistently beaten street consensus over the last six quarters, as shown in the chart below. Hence, it’s time to reevaluate the company’s profitability.

Expense Discipline Continues to Impress

The biggest reason why CCBG’s earnings beat my expectations was that the company showed good operating expense discipline in the second quarter. Although operating expenses grew in Q2’25 from the first quarter’s unusually low number, they did not rise by as much as expected.

Capital City Bank’s non-interest expenses had dropped in the first quarter mostly because of gains from the sale of banking facilities, as mentioned in the Q1’25 10-Q filing. Previously, I thought the first quarter’s improvement in the efficiency ratio was temporary and could not be sustained. As a result, I expected the efficiency ratio to bounce back up to last year’s level in the second quarter. However, Capital City Bank’s efficiency ratio rose to only 67% in the second quarter of this year, still significantly below last year’s level of 70%.

One of the reasons why CCBG was able to control its expenses was that it cut back on charitable contribution expenses and miscellaneous expenses, as mentioned in the Q2’25 10-Q filing.

I think that non-interest expenses will likely grow in the remainder of the year because tariffs will drive up inflation. Higher inflation will lead to wage pressure as the company will want to retain its skilled workforce. Inflation has been picking up pace since July, as shown below.

Consumers currently expect inflation to rise even further. According to the University of Michigan’s survey, year-ahead inflation expectations moved up from 4.5% in July to 4.8% in August.

As a result, I’m expecting the non-interest expenses to grow by 1.0% in each of the last two quarters of this year, leading to an average efficiency ratio of 68.4% for the second half of the year. This will bring the full year’s efficiency ratio to 66.8%, below last year’s efficiency ratio of 70.4%.

Compared to my last report, I have reduced my non-interest expenses estimate to $168 million for 2025 from my previous estimate of $174 million.

Weak Housing Market to Keep Loan Growth Low

After a small uptick in the first quarter, Capital City Bank’s loan book returned to a downtrend in the second quarter of the year. The loan book declined by 1.1%, while deposits decreased by 2.1% in Q2’25.

The second quarter’s decline was attributable to decreases in construction loans, consumer loans (primarily indirect auto loans), and commercial loans, as mentioned in the 10-Q filing. On the other hand, the residential real estate, commercial real estate, and home equity segments underwent some growth. Residential real estate is a key focus area for the company, followed by commercial real estate, as shown in the chart below.

Capital City Bank mostly operates in the northern part of Florida, with some presence in Georgia as well. Although the residential mortgage segment performed comparatively well in Q2, my hopes are not high for this segment because Florida’s housing market has been suffering. According to Redfin, the number of homes sold in the state dropped by 7.3% year-over-year in July.

Due to lackluster housing demand, home prices in Florida have started to dip. They were down by 1.3% year-over-year in July, according to Redfin. Georgia’s housing market is similarly stressed, with home sales down 4.1% year-over-year in July.

Fortunately, the business activity in Florida and Georgia is not too bad compared to the rest of the nation, as indicated by the unemployment rates. As a result, I’m expecting commercial loans to support the overall loan growth in the year ahead.

Keeping the above factors in mind, I believe there’s a good chance that loan growth will likely be low in the second half of 2025. I’m assuming loan growth of 0.5% for each of the last two quarters. Further, I’m expecting deposit growth to be close to loan growth.

Rate-Sensitive Loan Yield to Fall as Interest Rates Decline

Capital City Bank’s net interest margin continued to grow in the second quarter as the average asset yield rose while the average deposit cost remained stagnant. Going forward, I’m expecting the anticipated Fed funds rate cuts to be a major factor affecting the margin. Historically, Capital City Bank’s net interest margin has been positively correlated with the Fed funds rate.

The close correlation is attributable to the characteristics of the loan book. Around 44% of the loan portfolio will reprice within a year, according to details given in the earnings presentation.

Therefore, the average loan yield will likely drop soon after rate cuts. I’m expecting the Federal Reserve to cut rates by 25 basis points in September and another 25 basis points in December.

Meanwhile, the average deposit cost will not move much because it is already quite low. The average deposit cost was just 0.81% in the second quarter.

Overall, I’m expecting the net interest margin to be stable in the third quarter and then drop by 3 basis points in the last quarter of this year.

Raising the EPS Estimate to $3.57

As I’ve reduced my non-interest expense estimate, I will have to raise my EPS estimate as well. I’m expecting earnings to be higher this year compared to last year, partly because of the margin growth in the first half of this year. To arrive at my EPS estimate, I’m making the following assumptions as well.

  1. I’m assuming the provision expense for loan losses will remain near the average run rate of $1 million a quarter witnessed over the last year and a half.
  2. I’m assuming the non-interest income will remain stable near the second quarter’s level.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report an EPS of $3.57 for 2025, up by 15% year-over-year. Due to the non-interest expense line item, my new EPS estimate is higher than my previous estimate of $3.26.

Maintaining a Hold Rating

I adopted a hold rating on Capital City Bank Group in June with a December 2025 target price of $40.0. At that time, the stock was trading at $37.6. Since the release of my report, the stock price has surged by a strong 15% and shot past my year-end target. To update my valuation following the earnings revision, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (P/E) and price-to-tangible book (P/TB) multiples given below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $43.3 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $41.0 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $42.2, which suggests a small downside of 2.6% from the September 12 closing price.

Capital City Bank has recently increased its quarterly dividend by 8% to $0.26 per share. This dividend rate suggests a dividend yield of 2.4%. Adding the dividend yield to the price downside gives a total expected return of negative 0.2%. Hence, I’m going to maintain a hold rating.

Capital City Bank’s earnings have grown strongly in the first half of the year, thanks to margin expansion and below-expected non-interest expenses. Following the second quarter’s results, I’m raising my EPS estimate and my target price. Nevertheless, I’m not going to upgrade Capital City Bank Group’s rating because the recent price rally has brought the stock price close to my updated target price.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: Take The Exit
data.description:
Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (NASDAQ:WBD) soared to end the week based on rumors of Paramount Skydance (PSKY) planning to make a cash bid for the company. The entertainment conglomerate was planning to split the company into 2 units and this offer is intended to block that move. My investment thesis is Bearish on the stock based on valuation of the business, especially on a rip to $20+.

Potential Offer

According to media reports, Paramount is looking to make a bid next week for Warner. Bros. The stock already has a market cap of $40 billion and analysts are suggesting the deal needs to reach the $20+ range in order to really attract the interest of the media giant, requiring Larry Ellison to back the cash deal.

Warner Bros. has soared from only $12 a couple of days ago to end the week at nearly $19 already. The stock is up over 50% in just the last 2 trading days, so one has to assume a lot of the upside has been captured.

The deal faces a lot of issues from regulatory concerns to financing questions, considering Paramount is only worth $20 billion now. The media company faces massive dilution or debt on the balance sheet with Warner Bros. having a $30.7 billion net debt balance.

The CEO of Paramount is the son of Larry Ellison, worth upwards of $400 billion after the big rally in Oracle (ORCL), so the media company probably doesn't lack access to capital. The big question is whether the Paramount owners want the dilution, debt, regulatory, and integration headaches.

Over the last decade, most of the wealth created in the media space has gone to Netflix (NFLX). Warner Bros. has been involved in a lot of big transactions, and the company hasn't generated much value this decade, similar to Disney (DIS) and Paramount, while Netflix is up nearly 1,000%.

According to Nielsen, YouTube, owned by Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Netflix are the major media companies gaining US TV share. YouTube now owns 13.4% of the TV market, while Netflix is up to 8.8%, while both Paramount and WBD lost share in the last year. The combined company would reach close to the largest share at 13.1%, but if anything, this is market share for the leaders to take.

Just in the streaming bucket, Paramount and Warner Bros. are almost non-existent. One really has to question the interest in buying a company with just 1.4% of Nielsen's Total TV time via streaming.

While analysts forecast somewhere in the $3 billion synergy range, investors shouldn't expect a lot of value generation for shareholders. The company can cut costs by those amounts, but the only value is produced by growing the business with little benefits generated by spending time integrating the business while peers like Netflix and YouTube move forward with building out the business.

In general, Netflix created wealth for shareholders by growing the business to $45 billion in revenues this year to easily surpass Warner Bros. Paramount has to take the Warner Bros. business and grow it beyond the current point of nearly $70 billion, which is very difficult.

$12 For A Reason

Warner Bros. wasn't just trading at only $12 for a reason. If the company had a booming business, the stock would've traded much higher on its own.

For Q2, the media company reported revenue of $9.8 billion for a minimal 1% growth while missing analyst estimates by $20 million. Warner Bros. grew adjusted EBITDA by a more impressive 9% ex-FX to $2.0 billion.

In essence, the company is struggling to grow the massive empire with ~$40 billion in annual revenues. All of the bottomline growth comes from cutting costs, which isn't a winning story.

Warner Bros. still obtains nearly 50% of revenues from the Global Linear Networks while the Streaming business is up to $2.8 billion now. The key Streaming business is only growing 9%.

Even worse, the Global Linear Networks created $1.5 billion in adjusted EBITDA to double the Streaming & Studios segment. The Streaming business has definitely turned the corner with adjusted EBITDA going from a loss last Q2 to a profit this year of $293 million, but it's still a small part of the profit stream.

Warner Bros. already has 125.7 million global streaming subscribers. The domestic ARPU is above $11, but the domestic subs growth is mostly flat sequentially, so even this business isn't that exciting.

The big question is how the Paramount acquisition makes the company any better. The media company would spend the next year working on regulatory approvals (the Paramount/Skydance deal took 13 months to close), so one has to question the benefit here.

Ultimately though, Warner Bros. investors only care about what price Paramount might provide in a bid. The company is forecast to produce about $8.7 billion in adjusted EBITDA next year and trades at about 8x EV/EBITDA targets.

As the stock moves towards $20, Warner Bros. trades closer to 9x EV/EBITDA. The risk turns to a few dollars upside with $8 downside back to $12 on a bid failing to materialize due in a big part to the practical financing issues with such a small business trying to swallow Warner Bros. In addition, the regulatory risk could reduce the price the stock trades at during the lengthy approval process, limiting the gains from here for the next year, even on a deal.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that investors should quickly take any exit, especially on the stock trading above $20 next week. Warner Bros. has limited growth with high debt opening up the risk to falling back to the recent prices. Not to mention, Paramount would face a lot of leverage to even finance the deal, whether or the Ellison family has access to the capital.

data.title: Energy Transfer: A 7.5% Yielding Play That Is Deeply Undervalued
data.description:
In my opinion, one of the best opportunities on the market right now has got to be midstream/pipeline giant Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET). Back in June of this year, I explained why the company was my largest holding in my hyper-concentrated portfolios. Across my portfolios, it accounted for 21% of my total assets. Today, that number is a bit lower because the stock has fallen by 4.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 8.2%. In addition to that, some of my other holdings have appreciated. Even with that being the case, it still accounts for 17.6% of my total invested assets, and I have yet to sell a single share in months.

Even though the stock has underperformed since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate, overall performance since I first upgraded it to a ‘strong buy’ in November of 2019 has been stellar. While the S&P 500 is up 110.5% since then, shares of this industry giant have risen 138.7%. What's really great about the company is that, even with this outperformance, shares look cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. In addition to this, leverage is near the lower end of the spectrum, while the yield that the company provides investors is near the higher end. Add on top of this the expectation of continued growth as management continues to invest in growth opportunities, and to me this is a no-brainer that should be on everyone's list of top candidates.

A great business at a great price

As of this writing, the only new data that we have regarding Energy Transfer covers the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. At first glance, some of the results provided by management may look underwhelming. Revenue, for instance, contracted from $20.73 billion to $19.24 billion. But honestly, revenue really is not all that important in this space. More important would be profitability. Even on that front, some of the data has been less than ideal, with net income contracting from $1.18 billion to $1.09 billion. But when you look at the chart above, you can see that almost all of its cash flow metrics have risen nicely on a year-over-year basis.

The best way to look at this company is to look at each of its operating segments in turn. The largest of these, as measured by revenue during the quarter, was none other than the NGL and Refined Products Transportation and Services business. Overall revenue for the company grew from $5.80 billion to $5.94 billion. This came as most activities of the company performed increased. As an example, NGL transportation volumes grew from 2,235 MBbls per day to 2,331 MBbls per day. NGL and Refined Products Terminal Volumes increased from 1,506 MBbls per day to 1,553 MBbls per day. And NGL fractionation volumes grew from 1,093 MBbls per day to 1,150 MBbls per day.

Management attributed this growth primarily to higher volumes that the company achieved from the Permian region. The margins generated by this segment also grew, climbing from $1.28 billion to $1.31 billion. This was in spite of the fact that the company saw a $78 million decline in marketing margins caused by lower gains from its hedged NGLs and refined product inventories. It seems to have been driven, then, by the increase in activity, as well as a $33 million bump in transportation margin caused in part by contractual rate escalations for some of its pipeline systems.

Another very large part of the company would be its Crude Oil Transportation and Services segment, though its revenue plummeted from $7.37 billion last year to $5.75 billion this year. This was in spite of the fact that the company saw a jump in crude oil transportation volumes from 6,490 MBbls per day to 7,049 MBbls per day. This is not surprising, however, given that the increase in volume can be attributed to two additional investments that the company has made, which were more than offset by lower crude oil prices. Declines in revenue do not always translate to declines in profitability in this industry. But in this case, segment margin contracted from $801 million to $732 million. This was largely the result of decreased revenue, and higher operating expenses that the company experienced.

There are other segments as well. For instance, the firm's investment in Sunoco LP enjoyed revenue of $5.39 billion. But that was down from the $6.17 billion reported the previous year. Even though revenue for this part of the company fell, segment margin jumped from $320 million to $454 million. The company enjoyed an $85 million reduction in selling, general, and administrative costs thanks to the fact that, in the previous year, it endured a one-time acquisition expense associated with its purchase of NuStar. It also benefited to the tune of $48 million because of the firm's decision to contribute some of its assets to a joint venture in the Permian Basin.

Year-over-year, revenue associated with the midstream part of the company grew from $2.51 billion to $3.14 billion, with that primarily driven by newly acquired assets. That, and an increase in plant utilization, helped to drive up segment margin as well, with that figure expanding from $693 million to $768 million. It is worth mentioning that higher natural gas prices did help as well, but only to the tune of $38 million. In addition to this, we have its transportation and storage operations that include both Interstate and Intrastate activities. But these and other parts of the company are not as significant, and getting into that granular of detail might not be terribly instructive.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. This shows a decline in revenue, operating cash flow, and DCF. But outside of this, the picture for the business was quite positive. Net income, adjusted operating cash flow, and other profitability metrics, all improved on a year-over-year basis. Even though I would prefer that all profitability metrics improve, a mixed picture is not necessarily a bad picture.

Overall, I would say that things are going quite well for shareholders even though the stock has fallen in recent months. The fact of the matter is that management continues to make big investments in growth opportunities. This year, for instance, the company is planning to spend $6 billion toward capital projects. $5 billion of that will involve growth opportunities, with the remaining $1 billion associated with keeping existing operations running as they are.

Management believes that the midstream space offers enough upside potential in the long run to demand the largest chunk of investment. 30% of growth spending will be allocated here, with examples including processing expansions in the Permian Basin, treating upgrades, compression additions, well connects, and processing plant capacity additions. However, the company does intend to allocate 28% of growth toward its NGL & Refined Products business, another 28% toward its Intrastate Natural Gas Transportation business, 6% toward its crude operations, and the remaining 8% to its other assets.

In my previous article, I already covered a lot of the growth opportunities that the company has at its disposal. Examples here include increased energy demand for AI and data center operations, as well as the continued transition from coal-generated electricity to natural gas-generated electricity. I don't think it would be terribly helpful to rehash those details here. But clearly, management believes that the growth opportunities at the firm's disposal are robust.

For this year as a whole, management believes that EBITDA will come in at between $16.1 billion and $16.5 billion. However, they did say that it would likely be near the lower end of that range. If we scale up other profitability metrics to match this kind of growth, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow of about $12.65 billion, DCF, or distributable cash flow, of $8.12 billion, and what I refer to as ‘true free cash flow’ of $10.81 billion. For context, I refer to ‘true free cash flow’ as the number that we get when we print out from adjusted operating cash flow, which itself strips out changes in working capital from operating cash flow, maintenance capital expenditures, preferred distributions, and net flows involving non-controlling interests. This helps to paint a picture of the true health of the company in my view.

Using these estimates, and also using the historical results that Energy Transfer achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are incredibly low trading multiples, especially for such a high-quality cash cow of a business. In addition to the stock being cheap on an absolute basis, it's also cheap relative to other similar firms. In the chart below, I compared it to five such competitors. And what I found was that it ended up being cheaper than all of them.

To put into context how much cheaper the stock is, I created the chart below. In it, I calculated how much upside shares of the company would experience if the firm were to trade at the same multiple as the cheapest of these five firms. I did that on both a price-to-adjusted operating cash flow basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. I also calculated in that chart what the upside would be if it were to trade at the average of these five enterprises. On the low end, we get upside of between 64.6% and 73.4%. And using the average approach, we would get an upside of between 112.1% and 146.6%.

Usually when there is a disparity like this, there is a reason. To some extent, these multiple trading disparities can be chalked up to differences in operating structure. Some of these competitors, such as Kinder Morgan (KMI), Enbridge (ENB) and The Williams Companies (WMB), are C-Corps. But others, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and MPLX LP (MPLX), are also MLPs like Energy Transfer. And even with those firms, there is a definite and noticeable valuation disparity.

Another thing that is positive about Energy Transfer is the fact that its net leverage ratio really isn't that high. In the chart below, you can see that the company has a net leverage ratio, using estimates for 2025, of 3.76. This assumes that it achieves EBITDA that's at the very low end of its forecasted range for the year. Two of its competitors have net leverage ratios lower than what it does. But the other three, all of which are C-Corps, have higher leverage.

To make the situation even better, I have to note that Energy Transfer also has an incredibly high yield of 7.46% as of this writing. It used to be higher before the stock price appreciated nicely. And if memory serves me right, my effective yield was locked in comfortably above 9%. Historically speaking, C-Corps in this space do have lower yields than what MLPs offer. But as the chart below illustrates, one of the MLPs I compared it to has a yield that is meaningfully lower, while the other one is just barely higher.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I believe that the market continues to significantly undervalue Energy Transfer even though shares have materially outperformed the broader market over the last several years. I think that, fundamentally speaking, it is an industry-leading business, and the combination of low leverage, a high yield, and a cheap share price, makes it one of the most compelling opportunities on the market. Given this, I have no problem keeping it rated a ‘strong buy.’ And in all honesty, absent something major coming out of left field, the only reasons why I would sell it would be if either the share price appreciated materially from here, or if the market plunged and better opportunities became available. Otherwise, I intend to continue holding and will likely add more to my position as the economy moves closer to a recession.

data.title: Aurora Innovation: Too Many Red Flags Despite Promising Technology
data.description:
A lot has changed in the autonomous vehicle technology market since my first article about it in 2017. One of the most apparent developments, in my view, is that there is generally more clarity on which players have achieved progress significant enough to still be in the market in the coming years. For instance, the list of companies I mentioned in my original article includes Nvidia (NVDA) and Mobileye (MBLY) (even before it was acquired by Intel (INTC)), both of which are now generating significant revenue in the automotive market. Uber (UBER) and Google's (GOOG) Waymo also seem to be on track to bring more autonomous vehicles to the roads soon.

Aurora Innovation (NASDAQ:AUR), on the other hand, is a relatively new player in the market, as it was only founded in 2017 and went public in 2021. While the company has made impressive progress in its technology in a relatively short period of time, there are still no clear signs of whether it will survive in the long run. And with a high valuation close to $10 billion and almost no revenue to show for it, I believe it is better to stay on the sidelines until we see more positive signals for the company.

Aurora's solid list of partners and first tests are promising

One of the few positives for Aurora Innovation is its somewhat impressive list of partners. The company mentions that it is working with FedEx (FDX), Nvidia, Toyota (TM), Uber, and Volvo (OTCPK:VLVLY), among other companies. In theory, this looks like a great asset. If Aurora's technology becomes good enough, the company could quickly win big orders from just a few partners, thus generating its first significant revenue. For example, FedEx has a fleet of about 81,000 trucks, while Uber claims to have about 7 million Uber drivers worldwide. Just these two clients could potentially become life-changing for Aurora, which is expected to generate about $4.2 million in sales this year and under $37 million in 2026.

Aurora has also achieved significant progress lately with its self-driving technology tests, particularly with long-haul freight trucks. The company launched "the first driverless commercial trucking operations" (which still include safety drivers) in Q2 this year and has already logged more than 20,000 miles in that quarter, mostly in Texas.

These first tests are a huge advantage for Aurora. Logging actual self-driving miles is important for the AI-based technology to advance further, but it also acts as proof of concept for trucking companies. This means Aurora might start winning more customers in the coming quarters if the results prove to be valuable.

Generally, focusing on the trucking industry first might be a smart strategy, as a) the competition is less evident there, apart from the potential entrance of Tesla (TSLA), and b) the value proposition might be clearer. Aurora claims companies can achieve almost three times higher revenue and nine times higher profit per truck per week. These are significant cost savings that are hard to ignore, and the additional profits might also be captured by Aurora through subscription or service fees.

Some early partners seem to have switched to alternatives

What looks suspicious to me about Aurora's partnerships is how some early adopters and even current partners went another way relatively quickly. One of the biggest examples here is Aurora and Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY)—the two companies launched their partnership in 2018, and Aurora was supposed to be the technology provider for Volkswagen's mobility-as-a-service units. Yet the partnership actually ended in June 2019, just one year after the announcement. In June this year, Volkswagen's MaaS company MOIA revealed its production version of the ID.Buzz van is based on Mobileye's technology instead.

Uber is another example here. Both companies list each other as partners for autonomous mobility; Uber also owns about 21% of Aurora's shares outstanding. Still, when it comes to passenger mobility, Uber seems to have closer relations with Volkswagen or Waymo. Moreover, earlier this year, Uber issued $1.2 billion in convertible bonds tied to its share in Aurora, which appears to be a way for the company to potentially reduce a large portion of its stake without a major discount. Taken together, these factors make me personally doubt Uber has a long-term belief in Aurora.

Aurora's tech needs much more time to become valuable

Even though Aurora's first real tests with trucking companies are encouraging, they are just the first steps. It is clear that much more time and changes in regulation are needed for the tech to become as valuable as Aurora's calculations suggest it can be.

For instance, Waymo has already completed thousands of fully autonomous drives on the roads with about 100 million miles. Compared to that, Aurora's 20,000 miles look like a completely negligible figure. I believe the AI technology behind Aurora's self-driving system has more years of testing ahead of it to become viable and trustworthy.

These additional miles are also needed to persuade authorities to allow fully autonomous drives. Right now, an actual safety driver needs to be present in a vehicle during Aurora's tests, mostly because in case of an accident, there needs to be a human placing warning signals behind the truck. This basically negates all the potential savings a trucking company can achieve from using autonomous trucks, since the human factor is the biggest obstacle—a truck cannot be on a road and generate revenue while the safety driver is resting. The change in regulation is likely to come at some point, but these things tend to take a long time, especially at the international level. Meanwhile, there is no real opportunity for Aurora's customers to profit from the self-driving technology.

The company's financials show basically an empty company

Looking at Aurora's current financials, the company appears to be just an empty shell with no apparent signs of whether the concept can work. In the first six months of 2025, Aurora generated $1 million in revenue, with $5 million as the cost of this revenue. Because Aurora is just in a test stage, this is not particularly surprising. Still, I believe investors should know that buying Aurora's shares right now means they are buying a company that a) has revenue close to zero and b) might not be scalable, as its cost of sales is five times higher than its actual sales.

Moreover, during the period, Aurora spent $372 million on research and development and $65 million on SG&A, losing a total of $441 million from its operations in just six months. This might not be a huge problem for a big company with vast resources, like Google. Aurora, however, had just $206 million in cash and $1.1 billion in short-term investments at the end of Q2 2025.

With the current loss rate, Aurora will burn through its resources in 2-3 quarters. This means the company will probably need to issue additional stock to raise money (Aurora already issued common stock worth $427 million in the first six months of 2025) or take on significant debt, which would increase bankruptcy risk substantially. Either way, potential investors might lose a big portion or all of their money due to dilution or bankruptcy.

Aurora's valuation is too high

In light of all the points above, Aurora's $10 billion valuation looks rather surprising to me, even if I take speculation into account. With about $36.8 million in revenue forecast for 2026, the company's forward P/S ratio is 273. This is not to mention that, with the current revenue almost zero, the current P/S is close to infinity. (Seeking Alpha shows the 2025 P/S ratio close to 2,384)

Aurora's total valuation is close to that of another self-driving tech pure play, Mobileye, which has a market cap of $11.2 billion. However, Mobileye is expected to generate about $2 billion in revenue in 2026, which leads to a P/S of 5.6. Mobileye also generates significant profit, so its forward P/E ratio is actually favorable for today's market at 32.5. If we valued Aurora with the same P/S multiple as Mobileye, the company would be worth about $206 million. Therefore, the downside is too big, even for a speculative play.

As a bonus, Seeking Alpha Quant gives AUR a Sell rating with very low grades across the board.

Key takeaways

Aurora Innovation is an interesting player in the potentially lucrative industry of autonomous driving. Its solid list of partners and first tests of autonomous trucking are promising and might become strong assets in the distant future. Meanwhile, there are too many red flags about the company, like several partners switching to competitors, high cash burn, and an unfounded valuation. Therefore, I believe it is better to stay on the sidelines for now.

data.title: Power Business To Drive Solaris Energy As Oilfield Activity Softens (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Data Center Demand Fuels Growth

I discussed Solaris Oilfield Infrastructure (NYSE:SEI) and you can read the previous article here, published on September 4, 2024. SEI’s logistics segment is under pressure as oil price weakness cuts drilling and completion activity. Utilization is falling, fixed costs are heavy, and segment EBITDA is sliding. Power Solutions is the growth driver, helped by rising demand from AI, electrification, and grid resiliency.

The company is scaling fast with contracts, acquisitions, and the Stateline JV boosting capacity and visibility. While the climb in revenue and EBITDA is commendable, capex is depleting cash flow, and debt is moving up. The stock appears reasonably valued compared to its peers. Even with leverage risk, I think its strategies and drivers are robust enough to assign a "Buy" call on the stock.

Why Do I Upgrade My Rating?

In my previous discussion on SEI, published in September 2024, when I recommended a “Hold,” I wrote:

The upcoming MER acquisition is pivotal for SEI, given the sticky water it has recently found itself in. By incorporating a mobile distributed power market, it aims to add a revenue stream that can increase revenue per frac crew. MER’s impressive investment plan can generate higher demand growth across multiple end markets. MER plans to more than triple its fleet size capacity by 2025. As the energy market outlook deteriorated, sales decreased, translating into lower cash flow from operations. In connection with the MER Acquisition, SEI secured a $300 million loan and expects to obtain alternative financing.

After Q2, SEI’s logistics business is weakening with lower utilization and margin pressure. Power Solutions is scaling quickly with higher demand from data centres and digital technology. Revenue and EBITDA are rising, but high capex and debt remain key risks. Nonetheless, given the upside, I upgrade my rating to a “Buy.”

Acquisition Benefits Mobile Power Business

As I discussed in my previous article, in July 2024, SEI had announced the acquisition of Mobile Energy Rentals. The acquisition brought in a new mobile distributed power product line to Solaris’s offerings. MER’s power generation asset was expected to grow to 478 MW by Q3 2025 (from 153 MW) following the purchase of additional mobile turbines. Modular generation lets customers scale power gradually, matching capacity with actual data center demand. Investors may note that AI-driven workloads don’t need peak power from day one. At the same time, new regulations (Senate Bill 6 in Texas), can encourage co-located generation. The policy change can drive fresh commercial opportunities and enhance the value of distributed solutions.

Mobile power supply is evolving into a significant market. According to a Goldman Sachs report (as cited by the company), by 2027, AI server racks could use 50x more power than older cloud racks, with tech roadmaps already pointing to 1 megawatt per rack. SEI’s power solutions use turbines and reciprocating engines to cut time to power, lower emissions, and boost reliability. That way, it mixes technologies to match each project’s needs. Recently, it made SCR systems (Selective Catalytic Reduction systems) more mobile, which should speed up setup and reduce downtime on site.

Power Business and Strategies

SEI is scaling its power business quickly, growing from 150 megawatts in 2024 to over 600 megawatts across six customers. I expect contracts to drive further capacity additions in the coming quarters. End markets are broadening into microgrids, gas processing, utility grid resiliency, and AI data centers.

Demand is accelerating as electrification, AI, and reshoring trends strain the grid. The company’s strategy centers on modular, power-dense systems with hybrid microgrid structures that enhance redundancy and reliability. I think the company’s modular, reliable, and lower-emission solutions provide the company a competitive edge.

Recent Initiatives

Solaris has formed Stateline Power, a joint venture with a data center customer, to co-own and operate about 900 megawatts at one site. The JV will add $62 million in EBITDA to Solaris shareholders, which will give investors a clearer view of true profitability. In August 2025, it acquired HVMVLV to broaden its Power Solutions offering. The acquisition added electrical equipment, engineering, and design services to SEI’s portfolio.

Completion Activity Challenges and Risks

In Q2, SEI’s fully utilized systems (including sand systems and top fill systems) declined. It averaged 94 active systems in Q2, down 4%. The company expects softer crude oil prices to reduce drilling and completion activity. It can lower utilization by 10–15% in Q3. Because its Logistics segment involves high fixed costs, the segment margin can come under pressure, leading to a sharp EBITDA decline for the segment. I think the Power Solutions segment’s EBITDA growth will offset the weaker EBITDA of the Logistics segment. So, the company’s EBITDA can stay flat at $58 million–$63 million.

Over the years, Solaris has scaled from one system earning $1 million per frac crew to four systems generating about $4 million per crew. The completion industry trends point to fewer active crews needed to sustain production. Solaris’s sand system equipment should support the efficiency gains and higher productivity per crew.

I see low natural gas prices and crude oil supply exceeding demand weighing on the energy market’s outlook. As a result, its frac crews have been downsized. According to Primary Vision data, frac spread count decreased by 18% year-to-date. Rig count, during this period, declined by 9%. Lower completion activity and frac spread count can adversely affect SEI’s performance in Q3.

My Estimates

Over the past ten quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 17% on average. Given the impact of the drop in frac crews, offset by higher margin from power business, I expect the topline and EBITDA growth to accelerate moderately. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 15%-20%.

Analyzing The Q2 Performance

As disclosed in the Q2 2025 earnings press release dated July 23, from Q1 to Q2, SEI’s revenues increased by 18%. Strong growth in Power Solutions primarily led the rise in revenues. Adjusted EBITDA increased by 29% from Q1 to Q2. Lower fully utilized systems and an increase in lower-margin ancillary last-mile logistics services activity contributed to decreased EBITDA margin.

Cash Flows And Balance Sheet

In 6M 2025, SE’s cash flow from operations increased by 40% compared to a year ago. Higher revenues in the past year primarily led to the rise. Despite that, its free cash flows deteriorated and turned negative due to the capex on turbines and ancillary equipment to support the growth and operations.

SEI's liquidity was $195 million as of June 30, 2025. Its leverage (debt-to-equity) 0.5x was higher than its peers' average (PUMP, WTTR, and LBRT). As of Q2 2025, total debt stood at about $552 million. Its debt could rise to nearly $1 billion by 2027, though about a quarter of that will be borne by its JV partner.

Analyst Rating

According to Seeking Alpha, nine analysts rated it a "Buy " (including “Strong Buy”). None of the analysts rated it a “Hold” or a “sell." The consensus target price is $45.1, suggesting 49% upside at the current price.

Relative Valuation And My Target Price

SEI's forward EV/EBITDA multiple contraction versus the current EV/EBITDA is much steeper than its peers' average (PUMP, WTTR, and LBRT). This implies its EBITDA is expected to rise more sharply than its peers next year. This typically results in a much higher EV/EBITDA multiple. The company's EV/EBITDA multiple (13.9x) is higher than its peers' average of 3.8x. So, I think the stock is reasonably valued versus its peers at this level.

SEI's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 12.8x. If it trades at that multiple, the stock price can rise by 49% the current level. Since my last publication in September 2024, when I suggested a "Hold," the stock has increased by approximately 157%.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 15%- 20% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and applying the current sell-side EV/EBITDA multiple of ~14x, I think the stock should trade between $59 and $61, implying a ~98% upside.

What's The Take On SEI?

SEI’s Logistics business is under pressure as oil price softness reduces drilling and completion activity. Lower utilization and high fixed costs are weighing on margins, though Power Solutions growth is helping to balance EBITDA. Industry activity has slowed with fewer frac crews and rigs, but SEI’s equipment supports efficiency gains and higher productivity per crew. Near term, I see weakness in completions, but long-term demand for mobile power remains strong.

The power business is scaling rapidly, with contracts expanding capacity and demand rising from AI, electrification, and grid needs. Recent acquisitions and the Stateline JV broaden its technical capabilities and earnings visibility. Revenue and EBITDA rose sharply in Q2, but heavy capex pushed free cash flow negative. Its debt level has also mounted as capex on turbines and ancillary equipment increased. Despite the short-term hiccups, the Stateline Power JV and the HVMVLV acquisition can boost the company’s foray into the mobile power business and secure its long-term growth potential. I suggest investors “Buy” it with strong medium-term returns.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Duluth Has Changed Strategy And Is Working, But The Stock Is Too Optimistic
data.description:
Duluth Trading (NASDAQ:DLTH) reported positive Q2'25 results, the first after replacing its CEO in early Q2'25.

The results and the call provided important information regarding the company's strategy post-CEO change. I believe the changes, especially those related to assortment and digital strategy, are very positive.

Overall, I maintain the view that I have had on Duluth for some time already: the name is cheap compared to historical standards because execution has been very frail and because the company's cash position, while not dramatic, is not great. I believe this does not change in one quarter, and I maintain a Hold on DLTH.

A peek into a potentially successful strategy

I believe the company's results surprised the market, with the stock climbing almost 100% on the day after the results, because they show a glimpse of what could be possible under better execution. The company posted operating and net profitability, something that had not occurred in a second quarter for years. Most importantly, the company gained profits while losing sales.

Sheding unprofitable DTC

At the core of the new strategy is the abandonment of unprofitable sales, a topic I have discussed at large with ailing retailers like DLTH. Management teams sometimes decide that topline growth is the most important metric and therefore promote sales at the expense of profitability via enormous discounting and even larger expenses on online traffic acquisition. This 'empty calories' sales strategy is very common in DTC-heavy retailers, like DLTH itself.

DLTH has decided that it will cut unprofitable sales, even if that implies the topline declines. We saw that in this quarter, aggregate sales were down 7%, with DTC down 14% and stores up 5% (DTC is about 2/3 of the company's sales). The decrease, according to the call, occurred because traffic to online stores decreased due to lower promotions.

The first sign that the strategy might work is that sales declined 7%, but gross profits 'only' 2.3%, because gross margins expanded by almost 250 bps. That is, for each $1 of sales it lost, the company only lost $0.35 in gross profits. These were sales that were lower in gross profitability (30/35%) than the company's aggregate (~50/55%).

The next sign of success was seen in SG&A decreasing by $5 million while gross profits decreased by $2 million. The decrease in SG&A, according to the release, was due to lower outbound shipping costs (DTC shipping).

That is, the company lost $2 million in gross profits, for which it was spending about $5 million in shipping and SG&A. The net result is an improvement of $2 million in adjusted EBITDA and a flip from breakeven in Q2'24 to $3 million in operating income in Q2'25 (adding back restructuring in both cases).

During the call, the new CEO announced that the team is looking at gross profit dollars as the metric to follow. I believe this is correct, in that gross profits are more important than sales alone, but I would like to see a focus on operating income rather than gross profits.

Reducing SKUs

In previous articles on DLTH, I was always critical of the company's impressive SKU inflation. The company launched many varieties of the same products and was constantly introducing new categories, some of which were fairly disconnected from its core competencies (apparel for the most part).
