Positively, this quarter, the company announced that it expects 2026 collections to reduce the SKU count by 20%. Lower SKUs imply lower assortment expenses, better volume purchase discounts, and lower need to over-promote in order to clear slow-moving inventory.

Lessons from the past

The company's new CEO had been in charge of the company between 2015 and 2019. The period is illustrative of some challenges with retailing. During those years, Duluth grew revenues and gross profits because it was expanding its store base and gaining clients online. However, the cost was higher SG&A, which ate literally all of the gains in gross profitability. The result was a company that was larger but no more profitable. I hope this time management can learn from the past and concentrate on actual profitability.

Valuation is speculative given uncertainty

We have seen several retail turnaround stories in the past year and a half. They all showed improvements in profitability, some of which seem to have worked fairly well, like Citi Trends (CTRN), while others faltered after a few quarters of overperformance, like Children's Place (PLCE) or Torrid (CURV).

This is the main risk for Duluth today. The company is implementing a new strategy of discounting less and avoiding unprofitable (mostly online) sales. This strategy could play out perfectly from the get-go, with the company consistently improving its YoY profitability, or it may face setbacks.

The company could see its revenues decrease so much that even the gross margin gains are not enough. There are clearly other problems in assortment and design, and many customers have probably gotten used to discounting already (especially for a company that applied discounting so widely as Duluth).

As quarters build up, showing a consistent delivery of execution, then we can have more confidence that at least the internal factors are in the shareholders' favor. For the time being, we have one quarter only.

This is all in the context of a macroeconomic environment that is uncertain but where the bias tilts towards more challenges for consumer-facing companies.

Therefore, let us work with scenarios and see what is priced in.

If the company cannot improve permanently, then it generates no profitability at all, but losses, as it has done in the past two years. In that case, the value of the stock has to be very low, or even minimal, as an asset without profits is worth very little.

In an extremely bullish scenario, with the company returning to FY21 profitability, the name could generate $30 million in net income. This was peak Duluth, and believing it will be average Duluth going forward is a stretch of the mind, but for scenario purposes, let us consider it. Assuming a 10x earnings multiple on those profits, the name could be worth $300 million.

If both scenarios had the same probabilities, then we would have a 50% chance of $300 million and a 50% chance of $0 (if the company cannot generate profits, it is worth nothing over time). That is what the market is pricing today, approximately, at a market cap of $130 million.

I do not believe the chances of failure (current situation, sustained for several years, and driven by strategic factors) and the chances of impressive success (once in the company's history, during the best period for retail in the past twenty years) are the same. Therefore, I do not agree with this valuation.

If we go back to the 2015-2020 period, during which the company was under the guidance of the current CEO, then net profitability was closer to $18-22 million. If we use this scenario as the base, the probabilities between $0 and $200 million (P/E of 10x), for a market cap of $130 million, are about 70% for the 'historic average' scenario.

Still, I don't think a 70% chance of returning to that profitability is realistic yet. I believe the stock price has overreacted. Therefore, I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: Don't Be Blindsided By The Paramount Skydance Bid
data.description:
The Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) stock had its best day on record, surging 29% on September 11 after the Wall Street Journal reported that rival Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is potentially preparing an offer to acquire the company. The development adds a potential sale to WBD’s list of options, which also includes an impending split planned for completion by April 2026.

In the following analysis, we’ll provide an overview of WBD’s upcoming options—i.e., a sale vs. a split—and discuss their potential implications on the stock’s price outlook.

Paramount Skydance Offer

Wall Street Journal reported earlier this week that Paramount Skydance is allegedly preparing an offer to acquire WBD in its entirety, which consists of both the Streaming & Studios, and legacy Global Linear Networks businesses. Although the news remains in speculation, with neither company providing confirmation, WBD investors have responded favourably. The stock surged 29% on September 11, with upside momentum persisting into market open on September 12.

There are two major considerations on this news—namely, 1) potential regulatory hurdles, and 2) pricing considerations.

The first thing that comes to mind is the potential regulatory hurdle that Paramount Skydance and WBD could face if the two pursue a merger. This accordingly raises timing uncertainties over the closing of such a deal, which inadvertently raises execution risks as well. This is consistent with the two-year back-and-forth between Skydance and Paramount before the two finalized their merger last month. The concern is further corroborated by the extended antitrust reviews observed prior to the completion of the Warner Media and Discovery merger in April 2022, despite limited overlap in the two’s operations.

Since Paramount Skydance and WBD’s corporate structure and business model face significant overlap, a proposed merger could face heightened regulatory scrutiny this time around. Specifically, both companies currently operate two of America’s largest linear networks and direct-to-consumer streaming and studio businesses. This includes Paramount+ and HBO Max, which collectively reach almost 4% of total TV screentime. Paramount Pictures and Warner Brothers also host some of the world’s most significant IPs, including Mission: Impossible and Harry Potter, respectively.

There have also been industry concerns over the misalignment of a potential Paramount Skydance and WBD merger with the current Trump administration’s interests, given President Trump’s personal disputes with both companies. For instance, President Trump has long loathed WBD’s CNN for broadcasting “fake news,” while also having launched a lawsuit against Paramount for a deceptive interview aired during the elections last year that ended in a $16 million settlement. This accordingly raises concerns over the durability of WBD’s premature valuation upsurge this week on news of a potential tie-up with Paramount Skydance.

The second consideration that comes to mind is WBD management’s intentions for the company’s future. Specifically, the company remains focused on reducing its debt load, while also optimizing its Streaming & Studios business’ growth and earnings accretion amid intensifying industry competition. This aligns with the company’s recently confirmed intentions to pursue a split of the company by April 2026, following its organizational restructure into Global Linear Networks and Streaming & Studios completed in June.

Current CEO David Zaslav has also reiterated his optimism that the split should unlock a significant premium for the fast-growing Streaming & Studios business, as the majority of WBD’s current debt load would be spun out with the Global Linear Networks unit. As a result, for WBD to entertain a merger proposal from Paramount Skydance, the offer would need to at least match the premium that Zaslav’s looking for.

Since Zaslav is looking to unlock incremental value for WBD shareholders post-split, we believe a sum-of-the-parts valuation exercise could reasonably gauge the bid price that he’d desire from Paramount Skydance.

Our forecast currently expects WBD’s consolidated adjusted EBITDA, less corporate and intersegment eliminations, to expand at a five-year CAGR of less than 1%. This reflects the drag observed primarily in the Global Linear Networks business, which remains in secular decline and poised to impact the scalability of profit margins.

Under the SOTP valuation approach, we’ve assessed the industry median NTM EV/EBITDA multiples observed across each of the streaming, studio, and linear TV network industries. The multiple is applied to our 2025, 2026, and 2027 adjusted EBITDA estimates for each of WBD’s current Streaming, Studios, and Global Linear Networks operations to determine their respective enterprise values. These enterprise values are then adjusted for their respective portion of net debt allocated based on their share of consolidated adjusted EBITDA to compute the estimated equity value. The net debt adjustment is consistent with management’s expectations that the majority of WBD’s current leverage will be spun out with the Global Linear Networks business post-split. Our exercise yields an estimated price target of $10, $10, and $9 per share for each of the Streaming, Studios, and Global Linear Networks businesses. Adjusted for intersegment eliminations of $4 per share, the SOTP valuation approach estimates $25 per share for WBD.

Warner Bros. and Discovery Global Split

The other option that’s been in the works for WBD since December 2025 is the breakup of the company. Following the completion of its restructuring in June, which splits the company into Streaming & Studios and Global Linear Networks, WBD has announced intentions for their respective spin-offs to be completed by April 2026. Specifically, the Streaming & Studios business, which includes Warner Bros. Television, Warner Bros. Motion Picture Group, DC Studios, HBO, HBO Max, and Warner Bros. Gaming Studios, as well as their respective IPs, will be branded under a new company called “Warner Bros.” led by Zaslav. Meanwhile, the Global Linear Networks business, which includes CNN, U.S. TNT Sports, Discovery, Discovery+, Bleacher Report, and other “free-to-air channels across Europe,” will be housed under a new company called “Discovery Global,” led by current WBD CFO Gunnar Wiedenfels.

Given the WBD stock has performed better on news of a potential sale of the company than on management’s earlier announcement of an impending split, it suggests markets have reservations about the company’s future prospects. We view this potential skepticism as reasonable, given WBD’s elevated execution risks, which are represented primarily by the significant debt burden at its declining linear TV operations, and intensifying competition facing its higher growth streaming and film business.

As a result, we believe a fair post-split price estimate for the stock would likely land somewhere between the optimal SOTP premium of $25 per share, as discussed in the earlier section, and WBD’s current pre-split intrinsic value estimated at $14 per share (discussed in the later section). This accordingly yields a post-split price target of $20 per share, representing upside of 14% based on the WBD stock’s September 12 open price of $17.92 per share. However, to sustainably unlock this premium, it’d not only require the completion of the upcoming WBD split, but also consistent delivery on both companies’ goals—i.e., deleveraging for Discovery Global and earnings accretion for Warner Bros.

The Status Quo Remains

Considering the regulatory and pricing hurdles facing WBD’s sale option, and execution risks facing the split option, we believe the stock remains at risk of gravitating back towards its status quo price of $14 per share.

The $14 per share price target is computed by applying the median EV/EBITDA multiple observed across WBD’s comparable peers, reflecting the market’s moderate risk appetite for blended media and entertainment businesses that consist of linear TV, streaming, and film operations.

Since WBD is still at least seven months away from a finalized split—and much longer if it’s contemplating a sale—investor attention is likely to eventually come back to the company’s existing execution hurdles. This includes WBD’s ability to build FCF and move towards its deleveraging target while gaining market share in its streaming growth driver against intensifying industry competition.

As a result, we believe WBD’s current situation will put its upcoming quarterly earnings updates back into focus, making them key near-term catalysts for the stock’s performance outlook. We also believe investors will remain more grounded in their expectations this time around, and possess greater conservatism over WBD’s execution of its impending split or sale. The incremental cautiousness would reflect adequate sentiment, given previous market expectations for a post-merger premium unlock in April 2022 that had failed to take off.

Final Thoughts

While the potential Paramount Skydance offer for WBD has reignited investor optimism for the stock, we remain incrementally cautious of the recent upsurge’s durability. Although the SOTP valuation for WBD is favourable, unlocking this premium would remain dependent on continued execution on its deleveraging and FCF accretion efforts, as well as a completed sale, which could face significant regulatory challenges. Meanwhile, attaining the potential post-split premium would also rely on consistent achievement of market share gains to sustain growth at the core Warner Bros. Streaming & Studios business—the key purpose of the company’s restructure and decision to reverse course on the April 2022 merger. However, this remains a significant challenge given intensifying market competition.

Taken together, with consideration of near-term execution risks amid WBD’s extended schedule from its upcoming split or sale, we remain cautious of a value reversion to its status quo price of $14 per share.
